adolescent_relevance: TB burden can affect adolescents and young adults in many regions, and TB illustrates how exposure context and host immune genetics interact at the population level. GENARCH focuses on education and limitations.

exposure_modifiers.mechanism_hypothesis: Crowding increases exposure opportunities for airborne pathogens in population studies; outcomes depend on many contextual factors.
Nutrition status relates to immune function in population contexts; causality is complex and confounded by socioeconomic factors.
Ambient particulates can affect respiratory health and may influence susceptibility to infection; evidence varies by study design.

heritability_estimate.source: Host genetics estimates vary widely by exposure setting and phenotype definition

genetic_architecture.prs_notes: PRS for TB susceptibility are not broadly established and are highly context-dependent. GENARCH does not provide individual predictions.

top_loci.evidence: Innate immune receptor variation is studied in TB susceptibility; replication varies across cohorts and pathogen lineages.
Interferon-gamma signaling is central to TB containment biology; genetic evidence is complex and context-dependent.

name: Tuberculosis Susceptibility (Host Genetics Context)

population_equity.data_gaps: Phenotype definitions (infection, progression, severity) vary; exposure measurement and socioeconomic confounding are substantial.
population_equity.gwas_ancestry_breakdown: TB susceptibility studies are often region- and cohort-specific; ancestry and pathogen lineage context can strongly influence results.
population_equity.transferability_notes: Host genetic associations may not transfer across settings with different exposure intensity and pathogen strains. GENARCH highlights uncertainty and avoids individual-level inference.

references.title: Host genetics of tuberculosis susceptibility: mechanisms and evidence overview (review)
Housing conditions and respiratory infection risk: overview (review)

references.journal: Journal of the Air & Waste Management Association
references.title: Air pollution and health: evidence and mechanisms (review)

summary:
Tuberculosis (TB) is an infectious disease caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Population-level susceptibility and outcomes depend on exposure intensity, socioeconomic context, comorbidities, and healthcare access, alongside host immune response differences. Mechanistic hypotheses emphasize innate immune sensing, interferon and cytokine signaling, and macrophage biology that shape containment vs. progression.

Host genetic studies have identified immune pathway variants associated with TB susceptibility in some cohorts (e.g., genes related to innate immune receptors and interferon signaling), but results can be heterogeneous across populations and study designs. GENARCH presents TB host genetics as educational context for gene–environment reasoning and does not provide individual risk predictions or clinical advice.
